items.q: What did Adam Smith mean by the 'invisible hand'?
items.correct: That individuals pursuing their own self-interest in competitive markets can unintentionally produce socially beneficial outcomes through the price mechanism.
items.misconception: Adam Smith believed all selfish behaviour always benefits society.
items.random_wrong: The invisible hand refers to government regulating the economy secretly.
items.why: Smith was careful — the invisible hand works only under specific conditions (competition, no externalities, etc.). Students reduce him to a pure free-marketeer; his actual position was more nuanced.

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.

items.q: Alfred Marshall published 'Principles of Economics' establishing supply-demand analysis in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1890. “1776” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A fall in the price of a substitute good causes demand to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decrease (curve shifts left). “Increase (shifts right)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1890 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
